coredata.dc:title: Treating produced water - crude oil emulsion using concentric packed UV- bioelectrochemical cell (CPUBEC)
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Khlaif, Tahseen Hameed
coredata.dc:description: This research paper comes in the context of the growing global scientific path to develop clean renewable energy production methods that are commensurate with the required environmental standards, reduce carbon emissions, and treat pollutants in the water produced with the production of crude oil from oil fields, which are large quantities and are constantly increasing with the growing global demand for oil and gas. A bioelectrochemical cell was manufactured from available capacitive materials (GQDs/TiO2) with biofilm spread in the parts that are not directly exposed to UV ray in an innovative installation consisting of a cylindrical shell with spherical packing anode and a cathode in the middle of membrane that separates the tow electrodes. Both electrodes are arranged in a Pyrex pot and connected to external resistance. The packed cell (with and without uv-ray) performance was compared with unpacked cell to determined the effect of the packing and UV ray by studying the efficiency of removing pollutants TPH and COD. the best results in the packed cell with UV ray as follows:97.1 %, 97 % respectively, while the best results obtained in the case of using an unpacked cell was 86.6 %, 84.8 %. the change in the pH during the operating cycle was also studied and it was found that the change in the packed cell was slightly greater than the change in the unpacked cell as a result of the complex interactive on the surface of the anode. The highest current density produced was 1316 mA/m2 on the fifth day in packed cell and presence of UV ray.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Petroleum hydrocarbons
Produced water
Bioelectrochemical cell
Packed cell
Grean energy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is part of the scientific research program of the university of Kerbala, and the authors are grateful for the attribution provided by the Department of Petroleum Engineering and the University of Kerbala for completing the research. Tahseen Hameed Khlaif: Writing \u2013 review & editing, Writing \u2013 original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM (Scanning /Electron /Microscopy) images showing the Surface morphologies of the 0 f GQDs.
SEM (Scanning /Electron /Microscopy) images showing the Surface morphologies of the 0 f GQDs/TiO2.
X-ray diffraction analyses of GQDs, (define the disposition of atoms materials).
X-ray diffraction analyses of GQDs/TiO2 (define the disposition of atoms materials).
FT-IR Spectrum of TiO2/GQDs (shows the functional groups present in the nanocomposite.
XPS Analysis of GQDs/TiO2 (shows the surface elemental composition of nanocomposite).
N2 adsorption (ADS) /desorption (DES) isotherm to the synthesis 5 %-GQDs/TiO2.
BET plots (surface area analysis of the GQDs/TiO2).
BJH Plot (pore size distribution of the GQDs/TiO2).

UV–Vis absorption (measuring the absorption of the light in the visible and UV regions of GQDs/TiO2 and TiO2):(A)Tauc plot (shows the gap of optical band) (B) diffuse reflectance spectra (Analysis of optical properties of nanomaterials).
Show the change in TPH removal efficiency in each cycle: A) unpacked cell, B) packed cell, C) packed cell with UV-rays.
COD efficiency in each cycle: A) Cell with out packing, B) Cell with packing, C) Cell with packing and UV-ray.
pH affected each oprating cycle in A) Cell with out packing, B) Cell with packing and C) Cell with packing and UV-ray.
Open circuit potential of CPUBEC (opration under an infinite resistance.
Current Density of CPUBEC (operation under closed circuit).
polarization curve of CPUBEC (analyze the demeanor of bioelectrochemical reaction).
Characteristics of produced water (the dissolved solids in the produced water).
Textural properties of 5 %- GQDs/TiO2 by BET (Brunauer/Emmett/Teller) surface area analysis and BJH (Barret/Joyner/Halenda) surface area and volume distributions.
Biochemical tests and morphological characteristics of isolated bacteria.
Comparison of the performance of electrodes in MFC made from examples of PCM.
Comparison the types of MFC configurations.

head.ce:title: Treating produced water - crude oil emulsion using concentric packed UV- bioelectrochemical cell (CPUBEC)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Petroleum Engineering Department, University of Kerbala, Karbala, Iraq

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Petroleum Engineering Department, University of Kerbala

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Petroleum Engineering Department, University of Kerbala, Karbala, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research paper comes in the context of the growing global scientific path to develop clean renewable energy production methods that are commensurate with the required environmental standards, reduce carbon emissions, and treat pollutants in the water produced with the production of crude oil from oil fields, which are large quantities and are constantly increasing with the growing global demand for oil and gas. A bioelectrochemical cell was manufactured from available capacitive materials (GQDs/TiO2) with biofilm spread in the parts that are not directly exposed to UV ray in an innovative installation consisting of a cylindrical shell with spherical packing anode and a cathode in the middle of membrane that separates the tow electrodes. Both electrodes are arranged in a Pyrex pot and connected to external resistance. The packed cell (with and without uv-ray) performance was compared with unpacked cell to determined the effect of the packing and UV ray by studying the efficiency of removing pollutants TPH and COD. the best results in the packed cell with UV ray as follows:97.1 %, 97 % respectively, while the best results obtained in the case of using an unpacked cell was 86.6 %, 84.8 %. the change in the pH during the operating cycle was also studied and it was found that the change in the packed cell was slightly greater than the change in the unpacked cell as a result of the complex interactive on the surface of the anode. The highest current density produced was 1316 mA/m2 on the fifth day in packed cell and presence of UV ray.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The global economy is growing steadily as a result of the growth of various sectors, and the one of fastest growing sectors is the oil and gas industry,for which the demand is increasing, and with the increasing wastewater like produced water which polluted with crude oil residues [1]. The produced water categorized depending up on the source of production and the substance that directly or indirectly alter the nature of the water like hydrocarbons compounds, phenol, mercaptans, sulfide, ammonia, and other organic molecules [2,3]. These compounds can be found in highly complex forms in petroleum industry discharged water, and they are either directly or indirectly harmful to the environment. Conventional wastewater treatment processes consist of Flotation, Coagulation, Membrane separation technology, and Biological treatment, where its main goal is remove 0 f the significant volume of oil, after removing dissolved organic matter, suspended particles, pH, soaps, sulphide, ammonia, CO2 mitigation intensification and other contaminants [4,5].

Various technological improvements lag behind lots of factors that are not optimal and cause a large amount of oil to enter the water, resulting in pollution [6,7]. Therefore, it is important to develop a modern wastewater treatment technologies that, in addition to enhancing wastewater treatment efficiency, eliminate these challenges [8].

The concept of bioelectrochemical systems (BESs) is a new therapeutic method that relies on interaction between the electrodes as Donnor or acceptor of electrons with a surface layer of microbial [9]. Where compounds of economic value are produced in edition to energy while inorganic and organic substrates degrades, BES is considered a promising integrated system to meet the many challenges facing humanity and the environment [10,11]. this system takes advantages of the advantages of bacterial metabolism to produce electrical energy as a result of the chemical activity of the decomposition of substrates and liberation of power or gases at the electrodes [12–14]. If the purpose of the BEC system is to produce electricity, its called MFC which consists of two electrodes that are covered with suitable types of aerobic and anaerobic bacteria separated by a suitable barrier that allows the passage of ions through it [15]. During the microbial anodic process, it is widely utilized to remove substrates and generate energy. With similar anodic functions, the bioelectrochemical (BEC) system is thought to be the most similar to the MFCS (microbial fuel cell system) but, It is employed to produce hydrogen gas, depending on the outer power supply and anodic degradation of the substrates. [16–18]. To reduce carbon dioxide, the electrical synthesis cells (MES) system is usually provided with more power than the MEC system, resulting in industrially valuable products [19,20]. Furthermore, saltwater desalination can be done by BES technology using developed cell called MDC (microbial desalination cells) system [21,22]. BESs have been employed for various applications in recent years including generation of energy, creation of hydrogen, chemical synthesis, wastewater treatment, desalination, remediation, and sensing [23–25]. Although many other applications of BESs have been investigated, including the harvesting of value-added products (e.g., H2 and CH4), the removal of specific contaminants in wastewater such as heavy metals, and niche applications (e.g., biosensors), BESs have primarily been investigated in terms of two applications: (1) wastewater treatment and (2) generation of power [26,27]. Testing effectiveness of the developed Concentric packed UV- bioelectrochemical cell in treating oil-polluted wastewater and producing power is the main objective of this study.

The eco-friendly hydrothermal batch process was used to prepare graphene quantum dots from starch, which is considered a natural substance to achieve a path that takes into account environmental standards. This method is covered by the following procedures [22], starch and water are mixed under hydrothermal conditions in a single pot. the product was taken out After 120 minutes and using centrifugation to separate it from carbide and water.

To synthesis GODs/TiO2 using the hydrothermal method described in [23]. In short, 0,1 mg of GODs (graphene quantum dots) was placed in a beaker containing a mixture (5 ml) consists of a ratio of one part of ethanol to three part of ultra-pure water. Also 1 gm of TiO2 was placed in a beaker to dissolve in a mixture (50 ml) consists of a ratio of one part of ethanol to three part of ultrapure water, then a mixing process was applied to the two solutions until a complete homogeneity of slurry was obtained, the mixture was heated using stainless steel autoclave to 140℃ for four hours. The producing compound was separated by centrifugation from the solution and wash it several times with deionized water, after that, it was dried well by heating it under vacuum to 40℃.

To synthesize the electrodes we begin by preparing the anode, using 1 cm diameter balls and 15 cm diameter cylinder of nickel foam coated with the nanocomposite material (GQTs/TiO2) which was previously synthesized. To prepare the coating, 450 mg of the GQTs/TiO2 is mixed with 120 mg of carbon black and 30 mg (2 % Niphon solution) with 6 mg of a solution in equal proportions of distilled water and Ethanol. After mixing well use the ultrasonic device for half an hour to obtain a homogeneous solution. Then the prepared nickel foam was coated in its spherical and cylindrical shapes, so that the thickness of the coating is 3.5 mg.cm2 on a uniform basis, and it is placed in an oven for 15 hours to dry it well. The cathode electrode is made of carbon cloth brush with the same dimensions as the anode.

Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM Japan company)was used to study The structure and morphology of the synthesized GOTs/TiO2 and GOTs, X-Ray Diffraction, (XRD model JSM-7001 F) was achieved on Cu Kα (λ = 1.54178 Å) with a system of Rigaku Ultima IV and a scan range of radiation over 10° to 80°.

To measure the pore size,specific surface area. pore volumes and others of nanocomposite GOTS/ TIO2 by applying nitrogen(N2)adsorption desorption isotherms at −196 ℃. After degassed the samples for 2 hour at 250 ℃ [23,24]. Brunauer/ Emmett/ Teller test (BET MicromeriticsASAP2020, USA), The distribution of pore size was calculated via nitrogen desorption branches by a Non-Local Density Functional Theory (NLDFT) method. Fourier transform -infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was measured via instrument of infrared spectroscopy(Nicolet Nexus 670). TPH was analyzed by using standard method described in (APHA 2005). COD digester (LAN GE COD.UK).

heavy hydrocarbon materials and high salinity is the great challenge for microorganisms is in performing their role during the treatment of produced water by bioelectrochemical cells that requires bacteria with special specifications such as resistance to salinity and ability to produce bioservicants, in this study we was isolated a strains of microorganisms from a sample of Crude oil and test its ability to tolerate secrete biosurfactant and salinity. The direct method of isolation described in [25], In short, sterile containers was used to transport crude oil samples from the oil field to the laboratory at room temperature. to obtain bacterial strains we added Mineral Media (250 ml) to 500 ml container and put in it a crude oil sample (5 ml). two types of Mineral Media was used contains Bushnell Haas Medium and Raymond Medium, to which Normal Hexadecane (1 %) was added. This cultures was kept under aerobic condition at 40 ℃ (For a month), the strains of microorganism were isolated. the distinguished bacterial (after the incubation period), colonies were transferred to new plates of fresh agar to re-isolate them with repeat this process three times at least for the purpose of obtaining high purity [25,26].

The primary body of the CPUBEC was composed of a Pyrex glass cylindrical chamber (diameter 152 mm; height 100 mm), The anode electrode consists of contact parts the first was a cylinder made of nickel foam coated with the nanocomposite material (GQTs/TiO2). its external face was covered with a biofilm of isolated microorganisms and fixed to the wall of the glass chamber. the second was spherical packages made of nickel foam coated with GQTs/TiO2 and its lower half is covered with a biofilm of isolated microorganisms and sandwiched between the cylinder and the cylindrical membrane (a cation exchange membrane CEM) separating the two electrode chambers as In graphical abstract Fig. 1 carbon cloth brush was used to made a cylindrical cathode electrode (diameter 10 mm; height 100 mm) A schematic of CPUBEC is shown in Fig. 1.

The dissolved solids content (TDS) of the water produced with crude oil is usually high as well as a significant residues of petroleum hydrocarbons after leaving the primary separation process. the Produced water was taken from the Nasiriyah gas isolation plant and used as substrate in this study. Table 1 summarizes the dissolved solids in the produced water, The other properties is density:1100 g/m3, pH: 7 chemical oxygen demand (COD):780 mg/l.

The produced water is treated using innovative anode with biofilm which is obtained from isolated microorganism. The spherical packing in contact with the cylindrical shell of the anodes provide a large area and in the appropriate operating conditions (25 °C, inlet pH 7–8 and 5 total number of cycle operated for each case) we obtain an optimal study period for each cycle of 6 days in a batch system as a result of the drop in potential generation during the produced water degradation. Every six days 75 % of the treated produced water was replaced to start the next period (operating cycle), with each process of feeding or taking new samples,anaerobic conditions must be maintained inside the cell by purged nitrogen gas at a rate 50 ml/min for 10 minute. Samples of the treated produced water are taken at the specified time intervals for analysis to evaluate the efficiency of the treatment process by measuring the total petroleum hydrocarbon (TPH), COD, pH. with and without packing and UV light.

Ohm’s law was used to calculate the current generated during the process of treating the produced water by connecting the closed circuit with a series of resistors (100 ῼ), where the generated voltage is recorded on a regular basis. The power density was obtained by normalizing the power and current with the anode size and the current behavior was also monitored using several resistors to obtain the polarization curve to be analyzed and to know the maximum current and power density. the polarization curves were performed by applied different loads (external resistance) for a cycle, so as to achieve the efficiencies of treatment at various conditions of current, and to reduce the potential of power surpass [18].The external load were changed from 20,000 to 20 Ω in descending order during the overfeeding cycle. the reactor were left infinite circuit for a cycle. The CPUBEC was then operated with a finite external resistance (1000 Ω). digital Multimeter (model DT-830D,Kusam) was used to measure the Voltage potential and current with time at intervals of 30 min.

The electrochemical capacitive materials and design in this study were used to enhance the performance of the system to treat the produced water and generate electrical power. Capacitive materials have a large surface area, which makes it a storehouse of charges with electrical production [28,29]. GQTs/TiO2 is a nanocomposite material characterized by unique features that are integrated to produce an anode coating characterized by chemical stability, high surface area, many active sites that are more effective when exposed to ultraviolet radiation, high ability to adhere to the biological layer and electrochemical capacitive properties [30,31]. The design of the cell is in the form of two concentric cylinders separated by a protein-conducting membrane that gives a high surface area. It takes advantage of the geometric shape of the cell perfectly and provides a larger surface area for the anode and the cathode due to the need for a larger area to complete the reactions of the substrate that take place at the electrodes, which reduce overpotentials and local current density, while the total current density increases [32,33]. Also, the presence of packing in the anode between the membrane and the large cylinder reduce the distance between electrodes and gives Double surface area for the anode makes this system applicable as an example of scale-up for single cells. Microorganisms were spread and distributed on the bottom half of the spherical packing and the outer face of the anode cylinder to avoid direct rays that were directed at the upper packing half and inner face of anode, which was exposed to UV rays. This is because UV rays reduce the effectiveness of microorganisms and eliminate them if their intensity is high [34]. Also, UV rays have a major role in Activating the active sites on the surface of the coating of nanocomposite materials in order to optimally benefit from the features of light and microorganisms at the same time, which increases their effectiveness in treating produced polluted water and generating energy.

The SEM was used for investigate the nanocomposite (GQDs, GQDs/TiO2) morphology the images of SEM are shown in (Fig. 1, Fig. 2 respectively) indicates the size of the GQDs particle which arrange about (2.60–2.80) and diameters ranging (2.25–3.50 nm) with uniform distribution, (Fig. 2) demonstrates the structures of GQDs, where we can observe single, double and triple or lattice particles with arrange of lattice spaces 0.25–0.4 nm.

XRD (X-ray diffraction) was employed to characterized The prepared graphene quantum dots as shown in
Figs. 3, 4 which indicates a broad peak at approximately 26 A in a typical profile. The GQDs/TiO2 XRD patterns showed in Fig. 4B indicate a great similarity between TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 in the behavior which consistent with what was reported in previous studies [27]. This indicates that the TiO2 was decorated by graphene quantum dots 5 % with good distribution but it is not sufficient to change the structure of the nanocomposite, as it is not possible to observe any peaks or effects indicating the effects of GQDs on the nanocomposite crystal s structure [35].

functional groups was investigated by FTIR analysis of nanocomposites (GQDs/TiO2) as shown in Fig. 5. at 1067 cm−1 the C–O–C stretch was observed and at 655 – 802 cm−1 C – C stretch were assigned as bands, this indicates a loading of GQDs on TiO2 successfully [35].

The surface chemistry of The GQDs/TiO₂ was further examined using XPS analysis shown in ( Fig. 6) which identify the GQDs/TiO₂ surface elemental composition (C, O,Ti) and The peaks corresponding to Ti 2p has binding energy that is displaced from the standard binding energy of TiO2, which indicates the change occurring in the binding structure of the GQD/TiO2 nanocomposite due to the presence of GQDs and Ti-O-C as example bonding interactions makes the density of the electron greater and decreases the binding energy of TiO2 and charge transfer phenomena (between TiO₂ and GQDs). The formation of hybrid might in the GQDs/TiO₂ can be proven by the presence of the functional groups were characterized by absorption peaks in the spectrum period such as (288.21, 285.76 and 284.32 eV) are characteristic peaks to the (CO, C-O and C-C/CC) bonds respectively and in the period (532.40, 530.72 and 529.35 eV) which attributed to the (C-O, CO and Ti-O/·O2 groups).

N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm was used to measure the textural properties of 5 %- GQDs/TiO2 with more than one method which summarized in ( Table 2). surface areas, pore volume, pore size and pore distributions was measured using BET method, BJH method and isotherms desorption branch.

The unique features of GQDs and TiO2 positively affect the nanocomposites specific surface area, as the GQDs plays the role of a host substrate for amorphous of the TiO2 formation in situ and GQD restacking was preventing by TiO2, which leads to high stability of nanocomposites surface area

The minimum pore size of nanocomposites (GQDs/TiO2) is 1.21 nm,while the ranges of diameter of 2.5.Microorganisms between 0.5 and 2 mm, therefore bacteria enter the pores and this is very important so as not to cause blockages during their vital activities, which leads to death of bacteria due to lack of nutrients. The BET plots of the GQDs/TiO2 illustrate in Fig. 8, Fig. 9 illustrate the distribution of the BJH pore size we observed at pore dimeter 20 nm approximately the minimum pores volume change in relation to pores dimeter therefore we can classified it as super capacitive nanocomposites [36].

In the N2 desorption branch illustrate in typical curve and hysteresis loop Fig. 7 we observed the presence of mesopores significantly the total pore volume (of the 5 %- GQDs/TiO2) is 0.1432 cm3g−1. and the specific- surface area reaches 13.107 m2 g−1 . The ability of GQDs/TiO2 to increase acceptance of electrons from mediators and to integrate with biofilms of bacterial are positively affected by increasing the surface area of pores [37].

The optical properties of GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were examined using Ultraviolet-Visible Spectroscopy (UV-Vis) analysis shown in ( Fig. 10) To obtain comprehensive data for the visible and ultraviolet regions, a wavelength ranging between 200 and 800 was used. We can observed the difference in the band-gap between the TiO2 and the GQDs/TiO2 in the Tauc plot, where it was found that the band-gap for the TiO2 is wider than the band-gap of the GQDs/TiO2, this is evidence of the interactive effects and quantum confinement between the TiO2 and the GQDs [38]. it can also observed a red shift in edge of the absorption of the TiO2 when integrated with GQDs that leads to phenomena of potential charge transfer and more free carriers was generated which improves the activity of photocatalytic composite (when Charge transfer between GQDs and TiO2) under visible light [39].

In previous studies different types of technologies microorganisms brought or isolated from different sources were used to improve the performance of bioelectrochemical cell such as Chlorella vulgaris and nitrifying-enriched activated sludge [40], nitrite-oxidizing bacteria in activated sludge [41], Although there are many types of bacteria in samples of crude oil taken from the oil field, we isolated the bacteria kind P. aeruginosa because of their ability to degrade heavy crude oil [42], as they proved to be able to degrade 76 % of the crude oil within seven days in the oil handling test and increase in the growth of bacterial colonies, which indicates their adaptation to the environment in which they were placed. the biochemical tests and morphological characteristics of P. aeruginosa were identified as illustrated in Table 3.

The amount of TPH can be defined as the weight of organic fraction per one liter of produced water. The pw used in this study, which we obtained from the Nasiriyah oil field. fifteen operational cycles were used to address the produced water and determination of the TPH removal efficiency using the packed cell. five cycles without spherical packing, five with spherical packing and five with UV rays plus spherical packing in the anode. where it is possible to notice the highest removal efficiency achieved in the fifth cycle in both cases (packed and un packed anodes), but the removal percentage in the cell with the packed anode was significantly greater than its percentage in the cell with the unpacked anode as a result of the large increase in the area of the packed anode. The highest removal efficiency (97.1 %) was when the rays were shined on the cell, as it was the highest possible in the fifth cycle.as a result of the electron-hole pairs generation in a semiconductor nanocomposite when exposed to UV light. The organic pollutants were break down by the attack of the generated reactive species.

The thickness of the layer of nanocomposite material on the electrode was optimized to maximize photon absorption and minimize nanocomposite material. when the nanocomposite is higher than optimized level, the adsorption of light photon quickly declines as a result of particle to particle contact (agglomeration) and screening effect of light may be occurred, [43].

All installation modes of cell give varying performance in all cycles, as the removal efficiency in the cell with the packed anode for the first and second cycle was greater than the third cycle, which represents the lowest efficiency and the efficiency is higher in the fifth cycle, and in the unpacking cell, the efficiency was higher in the fourth cycle and the minimum removal efficiency in the third cycle, while the removal efficiency in the presence of rays is relatively close as a result of the synergistic effect of the presence of microorganisms and rays at the same time as illustrate in ( Fig. 11), without the performance of bacteria being negatively affected due to their lack of direct exposure to rays. This gives stability and constancy in the performance of the cell, which is a very important matter and is considered a step forward in the design and performance of bioelectrochemical cells that make them closer for commercial applications.

Starting from the fact that the TPH does not include all the organic content in the produced water, such as grease, so it is necessary to measure the substrate degradation by COD (chemical oxygen demand) analysis to the inlet and outlet samples from packed and unpacked (with and without UV-light) cells as illustrate in ( Fig. 12), which discloses all organic pollutants in the produce water. COD degradation in the unpacked cell varied in its efficiency during the five cycles, between the highest value in the first cycle, where the efficiency (the degradation of the COD) was 84.5 % and the rate of degradation was 3.5 kgCOD/m3-cycle. Also, the lowest efficiency of degradation of the substrate was 82.6 % in the third cycle, with a degradation rate of 3.0 kgCOD/m3-cycle.

COD degradation in the packed cell varied in its efficiency during the five cycles, between the highest value in the fifth cycle, where the efficiency (the degradation of the COD) was 94.8 % and the rate of degradation was 4.4 kgCOD/m3-cycle. Also, the lowest efficiency of degradation of the substrate was 91.3 % in the third cycle, with a degradation rate of 3.9 kgCOD/m3-cycle. The large surface area of the packed cell, the quantity and distribution of the biological material, and the reduction of the resistance between the electrodes as a result of reducing distance between them, was the reason for the improvement in the performance of the packed cell compared to its unpacked counterpart [44]. The highest removal rate was recorded in the fifth cycle when UV ray was present, which was achieved for the same reasons that were discussed in the previous paragraph.

It is recognized that bacteria are affected physiologically by environmental factors, and one of the factors governing the process of bacterial growth is the acidic factor, and thus affects the bioelectrochemical cell performance by affecting the mechanism of protein transport in the cell [13]. The produced water used in the study was equalized so that the pH of the produced water entering the process was neutral (pH =7) because it is better for the activity of the bacteria used in the process. During the five operating cycles, the change in pH was monitored as it was found that this effect differs significantly between the cell with the anode packed compared to the cell with unpacked anode. The highest pH of 7.3 was recorded in the second cycle and the lowest pH was recorded in the first cycle as 7.1 in the unpacked cell, while the highest pH was recorded in the third cycle as 7,7 and the lowest pH was recorded as 7.2 in the fifth cycle in the packed cell as illustrate in Fig. 13.

The change in the pH during the operating cycles was not a cause of the breakdown products of hydrocarbons because they do not give a basic or acidic effect. rather, the change in the pH comes as a result of complex interactions of types of salts near the anode, especially the sulfur compounds that result in compounds with a basic effect, and this means that the change in the pH it varies according to the composition of the produced water [35].

The generation of bioelectrical energy coincides with the process of treating the produced water pollutants in the cell, CPUBEC was initiated operation under an infinite resistance conditions for effective and uniform biofilm formation. The Potential produced with time was evaluated as shows in Fig. 14 the process took more than 65 hours to reach the highest potential 694 mV as a result to the bacteria response of microbial to the produced water salinity, the effectiveness and adaptation [44]. Then from approximately 65 to 121 hours, it remained at a high level due to the effect of the properties of the nanocomposite materials used in synthesizing the electrodes and their ability to store charges and then the decline in potential level began due to the internal resistance increasing and the consumption of substrate [45]

When CPUBEC operate under closed circuit condition where it was connected to an external resistance to determine current density with time as shown in Fig. 15 maximum current density was 1316 mA/m2 which achieved after fifth operating day and the polarization curve also drawn as shown in Fig. 16 by using various resistance {2000–20 ῼ). There are two important advantages in using an anode bioelectrode coated with an appropriate layer of nanocomposite materials that act as an internal capacitor due to its possession of the capacitor property: firstly, flexibility of the process due to its ability to buffer discontinuous current in a shortly scale and transfer on demand power secondly, an increase in energy density as a result of the high electrode specific surface area which decreases overpotentials and thus local current density, while the total current density increases. intermittent operation mode lead to power density increases for short periods more than continuous external load due to high counter ions and charge density stored in the GQDs/TiO2 [46]. t4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison the kinds of systems and reactor configurations
ce:section.ce:para: Many types of photocatalytic composite materials (PCM) have been used in previous research as a coating for the anode or the cathode in an MFC cell in order to improve its performance and increase its efficiency of removal the organic pollutant (OP) and green energy generation by increasing the electrode’s ability to transfer electrons and increasing the surface area and active sites. Among these materials are:

There are four common MFC configuration: Single Chamber MFC [53], Dual Chamber MFC [54],Stacked MFC [55], Upflow MFC [56]. each configuration has its own set of trade-offs involving cost, complexity, and efficiency(shown in Table 5). The choice of an MFC configuration largely depends on the specific goals of the application, such as whether the priority is to maximize power output, treat wastewater efficiently, or minimize costs and complexity for experimental setups.

The design of the cell in the form of concentric cylinders filled with spherical packing in contact with a cylinder to form the anode and made of the same materials that are characterized by unique properties such as their capacity, surface area, and ability to adhere biofilm to them gave the cell a great ability to achieve its desired goals and made it a promising system for commercial applications. The method of operating the cell also gave other features that improved its performance, as the UV rays were directed perpendicularly onto the spherical packing, the lower half of which was coated with biofilm only and the upper half was not coated, in order to spare the microorganisms exposure to direct rays, which weakens them and leads to reducing their effectiveness and eliminating them. As a result of the combination of design, materials used in synthesis, and method of operation, excellent results were obtained, as approximately 97.1 % of TPH, COD 94.8 % was removed and an electrical energy of 1240 mA/m3. Microorganisms were isolated from crude oil produced from the same field from which the produced water was to be treated to ensure their adaptation to the specifications of the produced water used, and this also helped it to gives these results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is part of the scientific research program of the university of Kerbala, and the authors are grateful for the attribution provided by the Department of Petroleum Engineering and the University of Kerbala for completing the research.

ce:section.ce:para: Tahseen Hameed Khlaif: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Tahseen Hameed Khlaif, Ali Jaafar Lateef,2022, hydrocarbons removal from oil water emulsion using nano UV TiO2/A C, AIP Conference Proceedings 2443, 030050, https://doi.org/10.1063/5.0091919.
